sentences.text: That was in Glewe.
And working on the company-- the transport company.
The war was coming to an end because--
Meisner, ?] [?
Meisner ?] Company.
Meisner ?] Company, as far as I remember.
You had-- you had to line up.
And you had-- we already had our places.
And they counted us if we were all there.
And after they finished counting-- and that was every morning.
They had to count to see if we were all there.
If something happens, if somebody didn't stay straight, or somebody came running in late, we might have been punished and stay outside, regardless warm or cold or snow or rain.
So we had to stay.
But depends-- there were the Germans coming up, and-- German SS.
And they were counting the people, and we were all there.
And then they were finished.
We went our way.
Most of the people in Glewe were a working company.
They all worked.
Different company-- nobody was in the daytime in the barracks.
The only one I remember was a lady.
She-- daughter was living above me.
She was working in the German camp.
She was cleaning the bathrooms.
She was the only one around.
And she finished there, and she was-- when she finished working, she was in the camp except that nobody was there.
An older lady.
Except in the infirmary, whoever was there-- the nurses-- except that, nobody was in the camp.
There was a guard all the time by the door-- by the gate that you couldn't get in and out.
And they were always equipped with-- they also had dogs with them.
And we had wire around-- around the camp.
And they were always walking around with guns.
They also also-- always had the guns ready.
So if you wanted sometimes to go over and talk to them, right away the gun was pointing at you.
You better stay away.
And at night, they did put on the electricity on the-- on the fences.
Still in the gray-- gray and blue dresses, gray and blue outfits.
For the longest time, I wouldn't buy anything for the house or wear anything with stripes.
Still to this day, I'm still shying away from striped things because it brings back too many memories which they're there.
If you want to think of them or not, they are there.
You go to sleep with them.
You wake up with them.
So you try to shy away.
But in a way, we are very lucky-- consider ourselves lucky that I'm here.
But why?
Why was I chosen from-- from my people, from my family?
We were a very close-knit family.
Why?
It is very tough.
You can't find the answers.
No, I don't think anybody has.
I just-- I just go by when you say on the high holidays, that we are written, who will die, who by water, who by fire?
And I do believe that our destiny is-- is written, and we are just hoping every year that we are put in the good book for life and health and whatever comes with it.
Maybe the best thing that you're doing is what you're doing right now.
It's not an easy thing.
Because I know after this, it will take me at least a few days till I get over it.
I'm just trying to give a message that--
From Glewe?
All right.
While we were in Glewe, we were in the field working.
And there were Americans.
There were planes coming.
And, of course, we used to pray for it, that they should drop a few bombs around us.
Of course, we were in the bunker.
They put us in the bunker when there was an air raid.
They put us in the bunker till the planes passed.
And then we had to go back to work.
We were told.
We were told that they-- the Germans.
Because, you see, the people who watched us were already kind of friendly to us.
I mean, they had a job to do.
They were not too happy with what they were doing either, but they had to do it.
So they-- some of us-- some of them were friendly.
And they talked to us, explained to us that that was it.
Here comes the American planes.
So we walked at night.
And we slept in the daytime.
They always found us somewhere a [? body ?] to sleep in.
So they took you out of the factory?
They took us out.
And they were taking us.
They didn't tell us where we were going.
But they just kept us going.
One-- we were in-- I think it was Berlin, another-- another big city.
And we just came there.
It was in the daytime.
And we just had planes overhead.
And they took us all in a field.
There was a field there, [INAUDIBLE] Field.
Some of the groups were going-- ran in the fields.
Some of it-- two other girls and myself went under a wagon with horses.
So we were lucky because somebody--
The Germans.
The Germans were taking us.
We didn't know-- they were taking us away further-- in-- deeper into Germany because the Russians and the Americans were close by.
So they kept us-- they took us further and further into Germany.
In fact, we were close to the Russians-- were closer to us.
We were like four, four on a line.
The only thing was-- the worst part of it was when a tank passed by.
The-- when a tank rolls on the sidewalk, it's such a deafening noise.
It's so terrifying.
Then they put us down in the end of the road and let the truck-- let the tanks pass.
German tanks.
German tanks--
--going back to Germany.
And what we saw on that walking on the road, it was the most terrible thing that you can think of.
While we were walking, there are also men walking.
And whoever-- the same thing with the women or the men-- whoever couldn't walk, they would not leave you on the road.
They shot you.
And you just saw on the both sides on the road, dead people.
And they just left them there, uncovered.
Some of them, the Germans had fun.
And they put them in potato sacks, and just lay them there.
And they put them in potato sacks, and they shot them, whoever wasn't strong enough.
So whatever help we had, we had to be strong to go a little bit further.
You make close friends.
You make close friends with walking.
As a matter of fact, I-- I made a very close friend during the walking.
And she saved my life until the days that I-- I don't know if I'm angry or I'm happy.
We became very close friends.
She was Hungarian.
That's all what I know.
She was [?
Bobo ?] from Debrecen.
And she already had a daughter.
The daughter she left with some people.
And she was there.
And we became very close.
On the road, they didn't feed us.
There we could get somewhere potato peels or beets.
The beets was the most delicious thing there was.
Or if there-- we drove into the city, and there was-- there was a bomb just before that.
And there were dead horses.
So of course, they did give us a chance to go and take a little piece from the horses.
That's the only thing which I said, I'd rather die than eat a piece of raw horse meat.
She was with me all the time.
We went into one of the camps.
I don't remember which one.
They were going to take us to Sweden.
I was already going when they took out some of the young kids.
Some of the people, whatever-- they took to Sweden.
That was the Swedish people.
They came in and they took out-- they took out as many people as they could.
The young people, they take them to Sweden from the concentration camp.
They came to the camp.
But it was already by the end-- the end of the war.
So they were letting-- I know some people who-- who were taken there, to Sweden.
I know they took them.
Then they them to psychologists.
They helped them out.
And some of them remained there.
They sent them to school.
And they came here.
I know quite a few of them.
They came to the United States afterwards.
They let them already come in because the war was coming to an end.
And I don't know why.
That's all that I know.
We walked in.
And I know I was in the barracks.
While we were already walking, and they told me, Rose, come on.
You should get my hand.
Let's run.
We're going-- we're going to Sweden.
So by the time we ran to the gate, they closed the gate.
They couldn't-- that morning, that was it.
They wouldn't allow them to go because the war was too close.
And from there, they took us walking again.
And that's when I was under the wagon, hiding from the train-- from the planes.
My friends pulled us out.
And believe it or not, when the bomb came, the horses ran.
Whoever was there, the-- the whole-- the whole wagon was demolished.
So somebody was looking out for me to stay alive for some purpose, I hope for a good one.
They came and pulled us out.
The-- we were with a group.
Where you stayed with people you worked, automatically, you make friends.
And we were all looking out for one another because we had no families.
That was our family.
So when they saw that we were stupid enough to get-- to go in the wagon without thinking of it--
--that it's a safe place, that they pulled us back in the field.
It was safer in the field because the bomb-- the bomb fell.
And it was a lot of, like, trees falling.
There were-- there were the Germans with the horses.
I didn't think Germans used horses.
But whatever it is, they-- they were with horses.
And there was a lot of dead horses.
I mean, we walked out.
It was-- I don't wish anybody to see that scene.
I mean, you've seen scenes in the movies, no comparison with what you saw there, a bloody street.
I think it-- I think it was Berlin.
It might have been, but I don't know.
It might have been--
--some other city.
No, they didn't tell us.
Nobody told us where.
And then we-- we were on that road for quite a while.
That was already afterwards.
That was one of the camps.
My husband told you last-- my husband told me a camp here.
But I just don't remember the name of the camp.
I have it written down somewhere.
But I don't remember.
Overnight, instead of sleeping in a barn, they took us into a camp.
They let us sleep in the barracks.
It was deep in Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, after another two or three nights, we-- we were liberated.
We just woke up one morning and the Germans were gone.
The Germans were gone.
And we just couldn't believe it.
This was the beginning of May.
I think it was something like May 5 or May 11.
I'm not sure of the date.
And that was, for us, the end of the war.
And the Russians came in.
The Russians-- the Russians were close.
They liberated us.
And they came--
I don't know, somewhere on the road.
It was still-- it was out of town.
It was fields, small houses.
It was a small town somewhere on the road.
But there must have been a farm because we slept over in a barn.
So the only place-- the barn could be big enough to put up so many people.
There was a German patrol going ahead at that time and arranging for us to sleep over to stay there.
It was all arranged.
They worked.
Well, they-- they needed us.
I-- the ones which left the crematorium, the ones which were there, they needed us for work.
They kept-- we were working for them.
Whatever they needed, our job-- so we were always there.
They-- so I don't know why, the only one which they-- they said, they shot by the road-- the people who couldn't walk.
And it's a shame that I don't remember.
After that, we walked-- which was tragically on the road.
That's when I lost my friend.
We came into a town, which, you know, the road, wherever you looked, there was all over our people walking.
So we came-- there was a little roadhouse by a-- by a station, a train station.
And they had a group.
And they said they couldn't walk anymore.
Let's go in and sleep over.
So we went in there.
We were maybe about eight or 10-- decided to sleep-- stay there, people-- we knew each other.
And they let us in.
It was empty.
Nobody was there.
The other group went further into town because they said that they have in town-- especially houses.
They went into German houses.
And, I mean, the Germans at that time-- the war was over.
They gave you anything you wanted.
They did anything you wanted.
I mean, you-- we walked into a cellar one time.
It was full with preserves and everything.
And she was just, take-- take anything you want.
Just don't-- just don't kill me.
So we didn't eat all those things.
And so a jar of preserves, that was something special.
Anyway, going back to the roadhouse, where we slept over, they came over-- Who's 'they'?
Russian soldiers.
Russian soldiers.
One was a captain.
And with them was one-- a Russian soldier, but he also spoke Hungarian.
They came over to see who was in there.
They were-- they were staying and talking.
Being that I was the only one who understood Russian-- because I speak Ukrainian, and I was able to talk to him-- so he says, I have to go.
That was in the afternoon.
I have to go.
I have to-- I have-- my shift ends at 12.
I will be back.
That's all what I want to do is sleep with you.
And so-- so here-- there came one of the soldiers, which was also German, the one who was born in Hungary was staying with us till 12 o'clock-- keep us company.
He was-- he a soldier.
But he was-- he was watching us.
So he says, as long as I'm staying here, you are safe.
It came 12 o'clock, 11:30, he had to leave.
He left.
And he told us, you know what?
They-- you-- they were like, to all the ladies, he says, get all the younger ones out.
So three of us pushed out through the window.
But my friend was kind of a heavy side, and she couldn't go through the window.
By the time we went out through the window and ran to a field, the Russian two soldiers were there already.
That old lady-- but she knew what was happening.
She took a chair and sat in front of the door and says, you can't come in.
The kids are sleeping.
To which, of course, he says, I don't want to do anything.
I just want to sleep with this young lady.
So finally, of course, they pushed her out of the way, they broke down the door.
And they came in.
And my friend, [?
Bobo, ?] she had tried to go to the window, to force herself, but they caught her.

Well, the three of us over there, all morning-- we were staying all night long in the fields, not-- not-- not being able to breathe, or staying low because we knew we were going-- we were going-- they-- they were going to look for us.
Well, when we came back the next morning, my friend was taken by ambulance to the-- to the hospital.
He couldn't get me, so he beat her up.
And he raped her.
But he did it so badly that they had to take her to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I went to visit her-- to the hospital.
And being that I was going back-- I was Czech.
And there was a transport from Prague taking back all the Czech people to Prague.
So she was there, says, Look, Rose.
I'm Hungarian.
And you do want to go-- you want to go back to Prague.
So you go ahead.
I'm going to be all right.
I have here two of my friends.
They will stay here with me.
She told you that?
Go ahead.
I said, so I go with you, whatever what will happen.
She made me go.
Unfortunately, I found out after the war-- I met those people after the war, one of them.
She died.
She-- she died in hospital.
And, I mean, she gave her life for me.
And here, I wish I could know more about her daughter to find out what's what.
Go and look for somebody in Debrecen.
It's funny.
We never really got-- we never got through to find out our second names or something.
See, while we were there, we were so-- just doing what we were being told, not thinking, what's happening, what's going to happen?
Besides, except like, singing songs and making up songs-- songs like-- there's a song in Hungarian, 'Don't worry [HUNGARIAN]'-- mother-- 'for my sister and myself.
We are all right.
Just look up in heaven and pray that the war should end and we can all go home.' So-- but we had to keep ourselves creative to a certain extent.
But somehow, with names, we never got to it.
So I really, I tried.
Some people-- after the war, I had met some people in Budapest.
And I tried to find out.
Sorry-- nobody could tell me anything.
So you still fear that-- you try to do your best, but you didn't.
Not you didn't-- you didn't know how.
I didn't how.
I don't know any other way.
It just-- sometimes things are just dealt for you to do it that way.
And there are some choices.
But when you are young, and when you just don't think, that's always in the back of my mind-- I want to go home, find my family.
So when she pushed me, when [?
Bobo ?] pushed me, I went.
Well, from--
Yes.
No.
The Germans took care of us till that morning, till the Russians came in.
Once in the morning, we walked outside and there was no Germans around, a half an hour later, the Russians were there.
The Russians took over.
We just assumed that that was it, that the Germans ran away.
We knew that Russians were close.
When we were already walking, we knew because we could hear the war in back of us.
We could hear the cannons.
And we could hear the shooting.
And we could hear the planes, the bombs.
We knew.
As a matter of fact, while we were walking and there were woods, some of the people which I spent most of the time in Glewe with-- there were two mothers and two daughters.
And another group, they remained in the woods.
They said, the Russians are here.
Why should we go in deeper to Germany?
But they met a very ill fate.
Because the Russians came in, and they raped their daughters, which the mothers had to stay and watch it.
So it wasn't such a beautiful-- beautiful sight.
I met those people in Kaschau after the war, which-- they were kind of sick.
So there were-- it wasn't a bad process.
I'm trying to make it light, not as gory as it really was.
I'm trying to modify it.
But it wasn't modified.
It wasn't nice at all.
It was treachery from morning till night, suffering.
But we tried to make the best of it, just hoping that tomorrow, the war will end.
And we will come out and find our families and start all over again, which is tough, still is.
To Prague.
As far as I remember, there were soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were speaking Hungarian.
Who they were, I really don't know.
If they were [INAUDIBLE], or there was something who took care of the people who were transferring them after the war, I really don't know who they were.
But I know we were in trucks.
They came back to Prague.
And we were in a camp-- also and they put us in a camp.
From there, everybody-- we could do whatever we wanted to do.
No.
No papers.
Nothing, nothing.
So from there, we looked around who was there, who was going where.
I decided, being that I was with a group from Kaschau, to go back to Kaschau.
They did allow us to travel on the trains without any money.
They were taking us wherever we had to go.
Whatever country we were in, they let us go free.
We just got a group.
And we went to the train station.
And they took us where we wanted to go.
Now, once I came to Kaschau, there was also-- we got together in one big place, all of us, we-- we met.
From there, there was-- I don't know how you would call it.
It was-- I think the owner might have already worked with them because there were places which we were able to go in and to see what's what.
We were able to get some food and sleep over.
It was just like a transit place.
From there, some of my friends did go to Sweden, six girls.
And being that I have found somebody who was going home-- so I decided that I'm not going to Sweden with you, which I wish I would have.
Because they went a complete-- they were there for six weeks with-- with psychologists, which helped them out a little bit.
So I went home to look for my family, which, with the train, there was also-- we always got a free ticket.
Sometimes you have to travel on the roof or sit on the floor, but we were always able to travel.
We're going to change tapes, and you tell me about what happened when you got back home.
OK.
Sorry.
